Consequently, trading volumes are projected to decrease as the holiday nears. However, the market is adequately supplied, as local converters indicate they possess sufficient inventory to satisfy current demand. In summary, the steady pricing trend in the market reflects prudent trading practices in anticipation of the holiday season, supported by ample stock levels among purchasers. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, prices fell on the back of weak buying activity as most market participants opted to remain on the sidelines in anticipation of fresh import offers from overseas suppliers. PS: Polystyrene prices witnessed a rise in some parts of Asia while quoting stable in other Asian region, this week. Reports of renewed progress in peace talks between Russia and Ukraine has eased geopolitical tensions, improving market sentiment and contributing to a decline in global oil prices. Concurrently, shipping companies are swiftly adjusting their strategies in response to a sharp drop in U.S.-China tariffs, which is expected to drive a surge in shipments. As demand grows on transpacific routes, securing cargo space on U.S.-bound shipping lanes has become a top priority for operators. This rapid response has led to a major rerouting of ships from existing lanes, indicating a broader reallocation of global capacity to meet shifting trade flows. The overall market prices showed an improvement compared to the previous week, influenced by recent significant fluctuations in the styrene monomer (SM) market. This volatility prompted downstream polystyrene (PS) users to closely observe and reassess market conditions. The heightened volatility in the SM sector fostered a more dynamic trading environment, leading participants to reconsider their positions and strategies, which contributed to a slight enhancement in liquidity across the Asian market. Most PS market participants remained cautious following the recent substantial changes in the upstream market. Despite these significant upstream shifts, such as price variations, supply interruptions, or regulatory changes, market participants chose not to commit to immediate spot transactions. This cautious approach likely arises from uncertainty regarding future market trends, potential volatility, or the intention to observe how upstream changes might affect downstream prices and supply chains before making spot purchases. In China's domestic polystyrene (PS) market, prices were predominantly elevated in comparison to other regions. However, locally produced goods remained more economical and competitively priced than imports. This cost benefit was especially significant for low-end applications, where price sensitivity is heightened and the demand for affordable materials is stronger. As a result, domestic PS supplies continued to be the favoured option for these applications due to their lower costs, even amidst the overall rise in market prices. In Southeast Asia, There was a slight improvement in spot buying interest in Southeast Asia this week, primarily influenced by the significant fluctuations observed in the upstream styrene monomer (SM) market. Some buyers, particularly those with limited inventories, returned to the market to explore cargo availability due to concerns that prices might continue to escalate, thereby increasing polystyrene (PS) quotes. However, substantial discussions remained relatively scarce. While the increase in SM prices led some buyers to re-evaluate their purchasing strategies, the prevailing uncertainty kept many cautious. Some opted to wait for clearer market indicators in the following week to ascertain whether the price rise would persist or merely represent temporary variations. Consequently, the conclusion of deals remained sluggish. The swift changes in SM prices also posed new challenges for regional PS sellers. Certain suppliers had to revise their price offers more frequently, particularly when SM experienced sharp movements within a short timeframe. A few sellers retracted their offers made earlier in the week, choosing instead to observe the SM trend further before reissuing offers, which further hindered the pace of negotiations. Although the rising SM costs may lend some support to PS market discussions, the degree of price transfer remains ambiguous. PS prices may not entirely reflect the recent gains in SM, as downstream demand has yet to exhibit a robust recovery. In previous weeks, when SM prices declined, PS prices demonstrated greater resistance to falling. This trend indicates that sellers may have limited capacity to significantly increase PS offers if end-user consumption continues to lag. Prices of PS in India have increased slightly due to gains in SM, yet they remain within a broad range influenced by Chinese and other Asian cargo prices. The demand for Asian cargoes has risen following the recent increase in SM prices this week. Domestic cargo prices remained stable this week, but they may experience upward pressure from the trend in import offers in the coming weeks. Market activity remained subdued as major importers maintained adequate stock levels. Prices were noted to be higher at the upper range, reflecting selling trends for Asian materials in the broader South Asian markets, while the lower range showed a firmer assessment in line with the overall price increase observed this week, influenced by stronger SM. Several sellers retracted their initial offers from earlier in the week, choosing to withhold until the following week to evaluate the potential for further increases in SM prices that could affect production margins. This cautious strategy indicates sellers' uncertainty regarding the short-term market trajectory, leading them to postpone their commitments. On the buyer side, those with limited inventories expressed interest in the market, acknowledging the necessity to replenish their stocks. However, their eagerness was moderated by a hesitance to make definitive commitments, primarily due to late-week price adjustments in SM, which raised fears of a possible price drop in the near term. Consequently, buyers adopted a more cautious approach, weighing the urgency to restock against the risk of incurring higher costs if the market were to decline. In summary, the market exhibited a prudent attitude from both sellers and buyers, with participants closely observing price fluctuations before proceeding with further actions. Reports of renewed progress in peace talks between Russia and Ukraine has eased geopolitical tensions, improving market sentiment and contributing to a decline in global oil prices. Concurrently, shipping companies are swiftly adjusting their strategies in response to a sharp drop in U.S.-China tariffs, which is expected to drive a surge in shipments. The Asian ABS market experienced a general upward trend, with prices increasing across various regions, fuelled by a resurgence in demand and optimistic market sentiment. The Southeast Asia marker maintained a slight premium over the China marker, indicative of stable regional demand dynamics. The reduction of tariffs by both the US and China has fostered a more advantageous trading environment, encouraging buyers to re-enter the market. Producers noted that Chinese buyers, in particular, have started to replenish their stocks, resulting in a significant rise in new orders. A producer from Malaysia pointed out that inventory restocking is in progress, while a Taiwanese producer confirmed heightened buying interest amid a sharp increase in feedstock costs, particularly butadiene. In South Asia, market participants expressed similar views, observing a gradual recovery in purchasing activity as regional converters aimed to secure materials in anticipation of forthcoming price hikes. The positive outlook in the wider Asian market, coupled with strengthening upstream trends�especially a notable surge in butadiene�has positively influenced demand in South Asia. PVC: This week, PVC prices displayed a mixed undertone in the Asian region. Reports of renewed progress in peace talks between Russia and Ukraine has eased geopolitical tensions, improving market sentiment and contributing to a decline in global oil prices. Concurrently, shipping companies are swiftly adjusting their strategies in response to a sharp drop in U.S.-China tariffs, which is expected to drive a surge in shipments. As demand grows on transpacific routes, securing cargo space on U.S.-bound shipping lanes has become a top priority for operators. This rapid response has led to a major rerouting of ships from existing lanes, indicating a broader reallocation of global capacity to meet shifting trade flows. In China, the export market for ethylene- and carbide-based polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in China has seen an increase in spot prices, largely due to the easing of tensions in the US-China trade conflict. This improvement in trade relations has created a more favourable market atmosphere, prompting producers to raise their offers. The temporary reduction in tariffs has played a crucial role in fostering this optimism by lowering export costs and enhancing international competitiveness. However, despite the rise in prices, domestic demand has remained weak, showing minimal signs of recovery. In summary, the increase in market prices has been primarily driven by geopolitical factors and tariff regulations, rather than by domestic consumption trends. Furthermore, Chinese producers are experiencing inventory challenges due to ongoing weak domestic demand, leading them to concentrate more on exports. The consumption of end-use consumer goods continues to be sluggish, and purchasing activity among downstream converters remains cautious, which further impacts local sales. Additionally, shipping costs have risen as Chinese producers prioritize exports in anticipation of the potential resumption of tariffs. The tightening of vessel availability and the growing demand for containers are contributing to higher freight rates, which in turn, are supporting elevated FOB values from China, especially for routes outside the US. In Southeast Asia, the demand for imports remains stable, as most purchasers are adequately supplied for May. The anticipated arrival of the monsoon season in the area is suppressing spot market activity, leading buyers to postpone their purchases and exhibit minimal urgency for spot purchases. Short-term purchasing is likely to remain subdued in anticipation of June offers from a major producer in the region. Freight rates are projected to stay in the near term, with vessel availability becoming constrained as more capacity is directed towards shipments bound for the US due to ongoing pre-tariff activities. The reduction in tariffs and a strong focus on exports may continue to support FOB China prices, despite domestic demand showing limited signs of improvement. In India, trading activity and negotiations concerning polyvinyl chloride (PVC) have been constrained mainly due to a lack of available market offers. This supply shortage has prompted most buyers to take a cautious approach, avoiding substantial transactions. Offers for PVC scheduled for June loading are expected to emerge in the near future, potentially invigorating market activity. Although the current atmosphere remains subdued, there has been a slight uptick in overall market sentiment. This cautious optimism stems from the belief that PVC offers will strengthen, bolstered by rising trends in domestic prices in China and increasing freight costs. Collectively, these elements indicate a possible tightening of supply and a potential increase in prices soon. The market has seen limited purchasing activity for products originating from China, largely due to ongoing shipping challenges and concerns regarding the timely delivery of shipments. These logistical issues have become particularly urgent with the impending deadline for Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) certification on June 24. As most Chinese suppliers have yet to obtain the necessary BIS certification, buyers are remaining cautious and reluctant to place larger orders for Chinese materials. This cautious sentiment is expected to persist in the coming weeks until more Chinese suppliers secure the required certification and shipping conditions improve, thereby restoring confidence in products from China. Looking forward, import demand is likely to remain weak due to the seasonal slowdown in downstream pipe sector activity associated with the monsoon. In Pakistan, The recent drop in prices can be attributed to several factors, such as reduced offers from sellers and heightened market pessimism, leading to transactions being conducted at lower price points. Currently, the market is marked by sufficient domestic supply, which alleviates upward pressure on prices. Furthermore, the anticipation of diminished demand in the coming weeks is shaping market sentiment. In particular, the forthcoming Eid holidays are expected to hinder downstream industrial activities, while the monsoon season generally results in a decrease in manufacturing and consumption. These seasonal and festive influences are likely to further restrict import demand in the near term, thereby contributing to the overall decline in prices. Consequently, producers elevated their offers to compensate for the increased feedstock expenses, despite the fact that overall market prices stayed the same. Market discussions have weakened compared to last month. A trader from China noted that this decline in activity is largely attributed to the rising costs of bottle-grade PET. As prices have surged, buyers have adopted a more cautious approach, leading to a slowdown in restocking efforts. Consequently, the heightened prices have diminished purchasing interest, resulting in less active market discussions than previously observed. In parallel, the markets for polyester yarn and fiber have experienced an upward trend due to increasing upstream costs. Market players have reported a slight rise in the operating rates of polyester manufacturing plants, with the utilization rate climbing to 94%, up from 93% the previous week. This increase indicates that polyester facilities are operating more consistently or at enhanced capacities, suggesting a potential strengthening in production activity within the polyester sector. The 1 percentage point rise reflects a modest yet significant recovery or stabilization in manufacturing operations, possibly influenced by factors such as heightened demand, favourable market conditions, or adjustments in supply chain logistics. Overall, the data indicates a gradual improvement in the utilization rate of polyester production facilities during this timeframe. Europe HDPE: This week, HDPE prices drifted lower in the European region. The existing downward trend in European high-density polyethylene (HDPE) prices is chiefly influenced by competitive material offers originating from the United States. The European polyethylene market continues to be subdued due to persistently low demand, with negotiations predominantly favouring buyers. Prices have experienced a significant decline over the past week. Injection-grade HDPE has been the most severely impacted as a result of a considerable influx of import offers from both the U.S. and Turkey. Although there have been sporadic supply shortages due to delays in anticipated shipments, these disruptions have had a negligible effect on the overall market dynamics. As the volume of market offers has risen, the price differential between HDPE blow molding and injection grades has also diminished. Market participants consistently characterize demand as weak�especially in the spot market, where buyers exhibit minimal interest in acquiring additional volumes. While demand in the packaging sector has remained stable, it continues to fall short in other downstream segments, particularly in the construction and automotive industries. Contract negotiations have been in progress over the past week. Some converters have reportedly pursued price reductions that exceed those resulting from the decline in monomer prices. Overall, market sentiment reflects a strong inclination towards further price reductions, particularly from suppliers who did not implement cuts in April and are now encountering heightened supply pressure. Consequently, many producers have become more amenable to negotiations. Several domestic producers have provided reductions that match the complete drop in monomer prices. Nevertheless, numerous market players are postponing final agreements until the month's conclusion, aiming to obtain more advantageous conditions, which has resulted in a limited number of finalized contract prices being reported. Producers characterized sales volumes in May as 'satisfactory,' although market dynamics evidently favoured buyers, many of whom achieved significant discounts relative to April. The price decreases were primarily linked to the reduction in the May ethylene monomer contract price, further exacerbated by ongoing weak end-user demand. LDPE: This week, LDPE prices tumbled in the European region. The European Low density polyethylene (LDPE) market has seen ongoing price decreases over the past week, mainly influenced by competitive rates from the United States. The LDPE spot market noted the most significant decline week-on-week, with above-normal import volumes from the U.S. entering Europe, contributing to increased supply. Free Delivered Northwest Europe (FD NWE) LDPE spot prices have reached their lowest point of the year, declining by 9% since the beginning of the month. LDPE has been the most vulnerable segment among polyethylene types, with reports showing a notable rise in supply entering the European market. Prices are falling swiftly as the surge of imported goods increases pressure on local manufacturers. The LDPE sector is especially affected, experiencing significant price drops because of competitive import deals from both the U.S. and Turkey, which have increased market competition. While certain expected shipments have faced delays, resulting in localized supply shortages, these interruptions have had a negligible effect on overall market dynamics. The price gap among polyethylene grades has also decreased as market offers have risen, contributing to the competitive landscape. Demand remains sluggish, particularly in the spot market, where interest in extra volumes is extremely limited. In downstream industries, the demand for packaging has stayed fairly stable, yet consumption in the automotive and construction sectors is not picking up, indicating wider economic difficulties. Contract discussions are in progress, with some converters reportedly asking for price cuts exceeding those warranted by the drop in monomer prices. In general, market sentiment indicates that sellers who refrained from applying discounts in April are now more willing to negotiate, influenced by the increasing availability of materials. Although some local manufacturers have executed complete price reductions in line with the decrease in monomer prices, only a limited number of contract agreements are noted to have been finalized. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices journeyed southward in the European region. Weak demand continues to prevail in the European linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) market. Coupled with abundant supply, this situation has perpetuated a bearish outlook and created unease among sellers, further exerting downward pressure on prices in May. The European LLDPE market experienced renewed downward momentum due to sluggish demand and heightened imports from the United States. This pressure was exacerbated by a ¬70/tonne decrease in the feedstock ethylene monthly contract price (MCP) for May, which further influenced spot prices. In spite of these developments, a clearer understanding of contractual pricing has yet to materialize, as numerous negotiations are still in progress or have not yet begun. Aggressive selling activity was apparent across all LLDPE grades, particularly from distributors seeking to offload excess inventory. These discounted offers are emerging even though May is generally a seasonal restocking period for various downstream sectors in anticipation of the summer downturn. U.S. export prices have stabilized following the announcement of a 90-day suspension on the implementation of additional import tariffs by both China and the U.S., after recent trade discussions in Geneva. This development has indicated a potential resumption of polyethylene (PE) export flows from the U.S. to China, which is anticipated to alleviate some of the supply surplus in the U.S. and may result in a rebound in U.S. PE export prices. However, it remains uncertain whether the lower end of European spot prices has reached its lowest point, as they continue to lag behind U.S. net forward PE pricing. The European PE market is still contending with the repercussions of prior bearish price trends, and the impacts are being acutely felt. Significantly, the gap between spot and contract PE prices in Europe has widened considerably, leading to increasing contention among buyers. This disparity has intensified hesitation in the market, as participants await clearer pricing signals in an environment still influenced by uncertainty and oversupply. Sellers have lowered prices for immediate volumes in response to increasing pressure from the anticipated influx of competitively priced polyethylene imports from the U.S. in June. To prevent possible inventory devaluation, some market participants are hurrying to liquidate current stocks, expecting lower replacement costs for incoming shipments. As the week advanced, the urgency to diminish excess volumes intensified, particularly among sellers with inventory levels exceeding expectations. This situation has altered the balance of negotiating power, favoring buyers, as many sellers seem more inclined to make concessions to finalize deals. Nevertheless, market perspectives remain polarized. Sellers with substantial inventory are implementing more aggressive pricing tactics, while those with more balanced stocks face less pressure to provide significant discounts. As the market strives for equilibrium amidst changing supply-demand dynamics, this disparity is generating a mixed pricing environment with the potential for additional volatility. PP: This week, PP contract prices quoted lower in the European region. In the European polypropylene market, contract prices experienced a decline this week due to persistently weak demand exerting downward pressure on pricing. Contract negotiations were reported to have concluded with reductions mirroring those in the propylene feedstock. The polypropylene market remains oversupplied, with increased material availability stemming from backlogged stocks from April, contributing to a bearish sentiment and causing concern among sellers, which in turn has led to further price declines in May. Despite some logistical challenges and isolated plant outages, these factors have had minimal effect on alleviating the overall demand weakness from key downstream sectors such as construction, automotive, and packaging�elements that have consistently reinforced the prevailing bearish outlook in the European polypropylene landscape. A decrease of Euro 65/mt in the May monthly contract price (MCP) for feedstock propylene has established a bearish context for polypropylene prices this month, followed by heightened selling activity as distributors engaged in destocking. The European polypropylene market continues to be characterized by low demand, mirroring the situation in April. The automotive sector is experiencing soft orders, exacerbated by a downturn in European car production due to the 25% tariffs imposed by the US on auto-part imports. Demand for packaging remains stable to weak, particularly in the BOPP segment, although not all market participants report weak demand for flexible packaging. A slight uptick in demand for caps and closures has been noted, which aligns with the seasonal increase in beverage bottle production during the warmer summer months. The cost of polypropylene (PP) has continued to face downward pressure due to a persistent reduction in production expenses. Following a decrease of EUR 55/tonne in April, the European propylene contract price fell by an additional EUR 65/tonne in May 2025. This extended decline in feedstock prices has diminished the pricing power of producers, significantly contributing to the decrease in PP prices. The ongoing surplus of Polypropylene (PP) has had minimal effect on the broader market. Buyers are advocating for more substantial reductions in instances where the disparity between PP spot and contract prices is relatively large, leading to increased contention among buyers. Producers are striving to maintain PP contract margins despite facing significant challenges. The European petrochemical sector is encountering financial difficulties, resulting in a series of asset closure announcements and ongoing evaluations of numerous assets. Consequently, many PP producers are adopting the position that contract pricing must remain above spot prices to ensure the operational viability of their assets and to guarantee supply security for buyers. The fundamental supply dynamics differ among producers and specific grades, influencing their pricing strategies. China's agreement with the United States to reduce trade tensions through significant tariff cuts by 14 May 2025, for an initial duration of three months, alleviates the pressure on China's substantial propane imports from the US, which are utilized for PP production at their PDH facilities. This development may alleviate concerns regarding China's PP production and could lead to sustained offers from overseas suppliers. Spot pricing remained stable throughout the week due to low trading activity, with the market experiencing minimal engagement attributed to a lack of demand for additional spot volumes. Material availability was robust, with no notable supply disruptions reported. Import offers were consistently available during the week from the Middle East and Asia, particularly South Korea. There were mixed reports regarding import pricing, with some indicating that forward delivery prices remained lower than spot prices for immediate delivery, while others found these arbitrages challenging to navigate. Following discussions in Geneva, China and the US announced a 90-day suspension of reciprocal import tariffs. On the operational front, normal supply has not yet resumed from polypropylene (PP) plants in Spain and Portugal, which were taken offline due to an unexpected power outage. The operational status of these facilities remains uncertain, and there have been reports of another shutdown at a site in Spain after a brief restart due to a detected leak in a pipeline supplying naphtha to the cracker. Additionally, a cracker in northern France continues to be offline due to an unrelated power outage that occurred in the latter half of April, although the nearby PP plant has been operational by sourcing propylene through imports. Furthermore, a PP plant in the Netherlands is set to begin a planned turnaround in the last week of May, expected to last until the end of June. In early May, significant logistical challenges resurfaced amidst compounding market difficulties. Despite these supply-side issues, the demand for Polypropylene (PP) continued to decline. Initial weaknesses observed in the construction and automotive sectors have now spread to related industries such as geotextiles, as consumer spending remains constrained, further exacerbating the bearish sentiment in the Polypropylene (PP) market. Buyers have taken a cautious approach, carefully observing the potential impact of the decrease in propylene costs on PP pricing. This has led to a gradual shift in sentiment from cautiousness to anxiety, as market participants await competitively priced PP offers. Looking ahead, European Polypropylene (PP) producers are likely to face ongoing pressure from both weak local demand and competitively priced imports. While quarterly contract arrangements may mitigate sharp price fluctuations in the short term, a further decline in spot Polypropylene (PP) prices is expected in the upcoming weeks. With ample inventories, buyers are anticipated to ramp up negotiations for more advantageous pricing terms, reinforcing the expectation of continued price softness in the European Polypropylene (PP) market. PS: This week, PS prices rolled over in the European region. European polystyrene market activity remained subdued during the week, as contract negotiations continued. Demand levels were stable, with limited buying activity for extra volumes and adequate supply to fulfill market requirements. Throughout the month, suppliers were reported to be offering discounts for purchases that exceeded contractual commitments. However, no buyers capitalized on these offers to acquire additional volumes. Buyers continued to take their contractual amounts but refrained from purchasing any extra volumes in anticipation of the June styrene contract settlement. In the downstream sector, there was a slight increase in construction activity. Additionally, concerns were expressed regarding the competitively priced polystyrene available from Asia and the Mediterranean regions. A source indicated that the sustained high prices in Europe had led to an influx of imports from Asia. Negotiations regarding contract prices for May have begun in the European polystyrene (PS) market, following a decline in upstream styrene contract prices observed last week. Discussions concerning distribution-level pricing are also anticipated to be in progress. The demand for May is expected to remain stable, consistent with the levels seen in April, with no major changes predicted. Nonetheless, minor fluctuations are anticipated across various downstream sectors. The construction sector is exhibiting seasonally heightened activity typical of spring, while demand in packaging remains consistent, and automotive demand continues to lag behind. Overall, the supply situation is balanced to long, remaining unchanged from previous months. Producers are keeping output levels in line with demand, with operating rates averaging approximately 60�70% of full capacity. Market participants expect that PS demand will likely stay flat and stable for the remainder of 2025, as downstream industries continue to be affected by broader macroeconomic factors, including trends in inflation and interest rates. Although inflation has somewhat moderated in 2025, it continues to be at elevated levels. Import offers from Asia were reported in April for May delivery, partially influenced by fluctuations in exchange rates. However, it remains uncertain whether this import competition will continue, as early May data and extended lead times restrict visibility. Furthermore, import offers from Egypt have also been observed since April. The European EPS market continues to exhibit stability, bolstered by several factors including seasonally appropriate and predictable demand, especially from the construction and packaging industries. The supply remains well-balanced, with production rates calibrated to align with current consumption levels. Feedstock costs are stable, encompassing recent manageable fluctuations in styrene prices. There is limited competition from imports, attributed to longer lead times and considerations regarding exchange rates. Buyer sentiment remains neutral, as the majority of market participants are purchasing only their contractual volumes. Unless there are unforeseen macroeconomic or geopolitical disruptions, this stable market environment is anticipated to endure in the near future. ABS: This week, ABS prices rolled over in the European region. The prices have remained stable as the European ABS market experienced stagnation during the week, influenced by consistent purchasing activity and stable supply conditions. Demand in the European ABS market held steady throughout the week, although there are some expectations for improvement. Despite the 90-day tariff between the US and China, ABS import prices did not change, as Asian manufacturers had already raised their prices to match those in Europe. Import purchasing was limited due to Asian prices being less competitive and overall low demand for purchases. Producers are finding it challenging to expand their profit margins in a climate of low downstream consumption, despite implementing production cuts. Overall demand remained weak, particularly due to poor performance in the automotive sector, which is a key downstream market. Additionally, ongoing stagnation in derivative sectors amid economic difficulties has led to buyers remaining cautious. Supply has remained robust despite a two-week turnaround from regional producers. From a demand standpoint, the stagnant automotive demand for ABS, driven by sluggish economic conditions, has significantly impacted the situation in the Eurozone. Uncertain demand forecasts suggest potential plant closures and an increase in ABS price trends, although some downstream participants are exercising caution. PVC: This week, PVC prices down adjusted in the European region. The European polyvinyl chloride market experienced a decline in both spot and contract prices during the week, influenced by competitively priced materials from Asia. This price drop was further exacerbated by lower feedstock costs, as producers sought to enhance their profit margins. Spot prices from Asia are significantly lower than those of domestic producers, which is constraining spot sales in Europe as buyers look for comparable prices that local producers are unable to meet. The global trade landscape has been affected by US tariffs and corresponding Chinese counter-tariffs, resulting in an influx of low-priced Asian offers in Europe, thereby capping domestic prices. European PVC producers are striving to absorb as much of the decline in ethylene prices as possible. Buyers are delaying their purchasing decisions, anticipating further decreases in ethylene prices later this month. PVC production is expected to rise following various planned maintenance turnarounds that commenced in April and are projected to conclude by the end of May or early June. The PVC market in Europe remains mixed, with some supply disruptions reported; however, the prevailing sentiment is that the market is oversupplied and PVC is readily available for purchase, which is hindering domestic producers' efforts to expand their margins against feedstock ethylene. Recent disruptions upstream due to a nationwide power outage a few weeks ago have affected some PVC supply at facilities in Spain, but normal operations are expected to resume soon. A facility in Poland is set to restart this weekend after a technical issue caused a temporary halt in operations. Additionally, a producer in Germany has initiated a brief turnaround this week, with plans to resume operations in early June. Concerns arose among market participants regarding rising freight rates from Asia affecting imports. Nevertheless, they asserted that competitively priced materials from Asia continued to exert some downward pressure on prices, although there were differing views on the availability of such materials between consumers and producers. Demand showed a slight improvement compared to April, as some consumers adopted a wait-and-see approach, anticipating lower prices in May, influenced by national holidays in April. However, significant seasonal demand from the construction sector had not yet emerged. Moreover, some sources expressed limited optimism about future demand, pointing to a lack of new housing starts and challenging macroeconomic conditions. In Turkey, a lack of buying interest negatively impacted market sentiment, and sources were reportedly less active due to the approaching Eid al-Adha holidays. PET: This week, PET spot prices gained, while contract prices were left unchanged in the European region. The European spot market for polyethylene terephthalate showed a growing preference for virgin PET over recycled PET during the week, influenced by competitive offers of virgin material. Market players highlighted the existing price differential as a significant factor driving the consumption of virgin PET in Europe, with some manufacturers adapting to the prevailing demand levels. The demand trajectory aligns with forecasts and mirrors trends expected for 2024. Overall, the demand for preforms has not met expectations; however, the current pricing of virgin PET is prompting changes in consumer purchasing behavior among recyclers. Additionally, European market participants are closely observing global upstream markets, as rising prices for Asian paraxylene and constraints in the US monoethylene glycol market are anticipated to affect supply dynamics in Europe. USHDPE: This week, HDPE Film export prices marched higher in the US. US spot export prices for High density polyethylene (HDPE) were evaluated as stable to slightly elevated across the majority of grades during the week that concluded, amidst increasing market discussions regarding a possible price increase in June. Market sentiment suggested that prices may have hit a bottom, with producers progressively testing higher offers. Customer inquiries have increased, indicating early signs of enhanced buying interest, although the overall recovery in demand remains uncertain. Several market participants conveyed a cautiously optimistic outlook for June, influenced by the recent reduction in US-China tariffs, which is anticipated to bolster trade flows and provide a much-needed lift to market confidence. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices were assessed higher in the US. US spot export prices for low-density polyethylene (LDPE) have continued to rise in the week ended, supported by indications of a slight recovery in demand, particularly from key Latin American markets. Market sources have indicated a minor increase in buying interest, as restocking efforts have resumed in certain areas after several weeks of low trading activity. The recent decrease in tariffs between the US and China has contributed a cautiously optimistic sentiment to the market. Although overall demand is still below historical averages, the reduction in trade tensions has enhanced the outlook for sellers and traders, particularly following the sharp price drop in April. Many participants in the market across the US and Latin America perceive the tariff relief as a possible turning point that could bolster export volumes and stabilize pricing trends. However, the market is still far from achieving balance. An ongoing oversupply of US-origin materials, combined with elevated inventories at downstream processors, continues to apply downward pressure on market fundamentals. Some traders have observed that, despite the improved sentiment, purchasing activity remains selective, with buyers reluctant to commit to large quantities until more definitive signs of a sustained price recovery are evident. Nonetheless, an increasing number of sellers are maintaining firm prices or attempting slight increases, citing limited potential for further declines. Should demand continue to strengthen and inventory pressures diminish, additional gains may be anticipated in the upcoming weeks. Nevertheless, uncertainty regarding global economic conditions and the temporary nature of the US-China tariff truce moderates any excessively optimistic forecasts. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices were assessed steady in the US. Participants in the LLDPE market throughout Latin America and the United States have cautiously received the recent decrease in tariffs imposed by China, perceiving it as a potentially favorable turning point after a significant decline in prices. Although the general sentiment continues to be mixed due to persistent market uncertainties and an ongoing oversupply of US-origin materials, certain traders have conveyed a sense of cautious optimism that the relief from tariffs may encourage demand and facilitate gradual price increases in the weeks ahead. PP: This week, PP export prices witnessed sharp gains in the US. US spot export polypropylene (PP) prices experienced a slight increase in the week concluded, bolstered by a rise in demand from Mexico, which has revitalized an otherwise lacklustre export market. Buyers in the area have sought to secure volumes in light of increasing expectations that the current price levels may not last long. The recent surge in demand from Mexico, a key destination for US PP exports, along with the resumption of US propane shipments to China after the reduction of bilateral tariffs, has fostered a more positive pricing outlook. Market participants are of the opinion that these developments could propel PP prices higher in the upcoming weeks, despite ongoing global competition and concerns regarding supply overhang. PS: This week, PS export prices were left unchanged in the US. Prices for spot export polystyrene (PS) remained unchanged during the week that concluded, while spot import prices for expandable polystyrene (EPS) experienced an increase. The stability of PS prices was noted across major global regions, including Asia, Africa, and Europe, where consistent supply and demand fundamentals contributed to a balanced market. In the upstream sector, the benzene market also exhibited stability, amidst discussions regarding decreased import volumes into the US, which assisted in sustaining equilibrium in feedstock pricing. Within the PS market, industry participants observed a potential opportunity arising due to escalating freight rates from Asia, which could render alternative sourcing regions more competitive and alter trade dynamics in the near future. PVC: This week, PVC export prices quoted flat in the US. The export market for polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in the United States has remained subdued, with minimal activity noted as the majority of market participants awaited new offers for June. This cautious stance resulted in price stability over the course of the week. Market feedback indicates that only one producer was actively involved in negotiations, while most traders and distributors remained inactive. Despite the prevailing quietness, there seems to be a gradual improvement in sentiment. A handful of volume inquiries have emerged, yet most suppliers are choosing to postpone firm negotiations until next month, reflecting a wait-and-see strategy in light of changing market conditions. PET: This week, PET prices rolled over in the US. Prices in the US spot import market for polyethylene terephthalate (PET) remained stable throughout the week, despite the ongoing increase in ocean freight rates. Market participants observed that high freight costs, along with prolonged transit times, have resulted in logistical difficulties, especially for importers dependent on shipments from Asia and the Middle East. Both the virgin and recycled PET sectors are facing a climate of persistent uncertainty, as trade disputes and wider economic challenges are impacting market sentiment. Sources indicate that these elements are shaping procurement strategies, with buyers becoming more cautious and emphasizing cost efficiency and delivery reliability in the context of fluctuating global trade conditions.

data.title: Estate Planning Is Tricky. How Volatility Can Actually Help. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Debbie Carlson

Volatility makes estate planning precarious since markets can pivot while you are deciding specifics that could have ramifications for years to come.

Yet savvy planning can allow you to take advantage of market swings to enhance future returns. Most people think about gifting and estate-planning strategies when markets are on an upswing, but some of the most effective estate planning occurs when markets are in the dumps and assets have bargain-basement prices.

Stocks are top of mind for investors now because of April's brutal drop and the subsequent rebound. But smart estate planning can be used on any assets that have fallen in value and are expected to see higher growth.

Consider Asset Size Rather Than Current Value

Most people think about current value when considering estate-planning strategies. But when markets are volatile, it makes more sense to think about the total number of shares rather than price.

The annual federal gift-tax exemption for 2025 is $19,000, and with lower asset prices you can give more shares before hitting that number, notes Krysta Dos Santos, head of financial planning at GenTrust. That allows recipients to benefit from any future rebound in the share price.

People trying to stay within the lifetime federal estate-tax exemption of nearly $14 million per person also benefit from depleted assets. For example, if a business that was once worth $15 million is only worth $14 million when a donor puts it inside a trust, he or she avoids estate taxes on $1 million above the lifetime exemption. And any growth in the business value will go tax-free to heirs.

"We never know what the market will do, but in theory that recovery is going to happen outside of your estate, and all of that is protected, not just the amount gifted, but any growth," Dos Santos says.

There are potential changes in lifetime gifting levels as Congress hammers out a new spending bill. The House of Representatives has voted to make permanent the 2017 Trump tax cuts, known as the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, but the Senate is still in negotiations. If the current estate tax exemptions aren't extended, the tax exemption will revert to previous levels, which would be around $6.8 million adjusted for inflation.

Other Options to Consider

If you have used up your lifetime exemption but still want to avoid wealth transfer taxes for gifting, grantor retained annuity trusts, or GRATs, are an option. They aren't affected by lifetime exemptions.

Evan Rothschild, senior wealth advisor at Focus Partners Wealth, says GRATs are irrevocable temporary trusts with short terms, such as two to five years. Grantors transfer assets and receive annuity payments over the time frame. If the asset grows in value, beneficiaries receive the balance of the trust's assets when the term ends, free of gift and estate taxes.

"It's basically a loan to a trust. You're using stock as the asset that you're giving, instead of cash," Rothschild says.

GRATS are most popular when interest rates are low because the Internal Revenue Service sets the annuity payment rate at 120% of applicable federal rate midterm rate, which currently puts the payment rate at 5%. Asset growth needs to get above that hurdle rate to exceed the annuity payment. If the GRAT fails to exceed the hurdle rate, your heirs won't get any money from it.

Dos Santos says although interest rates aren't particularly low, using depressed assets can help the GRAT exceed the hurdle rate. "You're coming from a low spot and expect there to be some rebound that they can participate," she says.

The advisors say because estate-planning strategies to reduce taxes involve irrevocable trusts, people shouldn't be rash about these decisions. Once you have put money in them, you can't get it back.

Not all estate planning has to be complex. Dos Santos worked with a 90-year-old client to convert a $5 million traditional tax-deferred individual retirement account to a Roth IRA while stock values were down. By paying the tax on the $5 million conversion now, the client is lowering the size of her taxable estate, and the entire Roth IRS will go to her son tax-free. That includes any rebound in the battered shares placed in the Roth IRA.

"This is...a very legitimate thing, and there's plenty of elderly people that might fall into this bucket" of a high taxable estate, she says.

Write to editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Saudi Aramco considers asset sales to free up funds, sources say
data.description:
Saudi state oil giant Aramco is exploring potential asset sales to free up funds, two people with knowledge of the matter said, as it pursues an international expansion and weathers lower crude prices.

Aramco is the world's largest oil-producing company and the main source of Saudi state revenue. The firm will slash dividend payouts by nearly a third this year as lower oil prices hit its income.

The company has asked investment bankers to pitch ideas for how to raise funds from its assets, the people said.

They declined to say which assets could be sold or name the banks involved.

Aramco declined to comment.

Aramco is looking to improve efficiency and cut costs, according to two other people with knowledge of the matter, and an option under consideration would be asset sales, one of them said. The four sources declined to be named because they are not authorised to speak to media.

Aramco is the engine of the Saudi economy and its sprawling business includes units for aviation, construction and sports. It has retained majority stakes during previous asset sales such as its deals around its pipeline infrastructure.

The Saudi government is putting pressure on its industries to improve profitability amid low crude prices and as it spends its hydrocarbon wealth on new sectors to cut reliance on oil.

The kingdom faces a widening budget deficit with the International Monetary Fund saying Riyadh needs a price of oil of over $90 per barrel to balance its books compared to prices of around $60 per barrel in recent weeks.

Aramco has in recent years made a push to grow its global footprint, including investing in Chinese refineries, Chilean fuel retailer Esmax and U.S.-headquartered LNG firm MidOcean.

The Saudi company said last week it signed 34 preliminary deals potentially worth up to $90 billion with U.S. firms following President Donald Trump's visit to the kingdom.

data.title: India's monsoon rains arrive 8 days early, earliest in 16 years
data.description:
Monsoon rains hit the coast of India's southernmost state of Kerala on Saturday, eight days earlier than usual, marking the earliest arrival in 16 years and providing the promise of a bumper harvest and relief from a gruelling heatwave.

The monsoon, the lifeblood of the country's $4 trillion economy, delivers nearly 70% of the rain that India needs to water farms and replenish aquifers and reservoirs. Nearly half of India's farmland, without any irrigation cover, depends on the annual June-September rains to grow a number of crops.

Summer rains usually begin to lash Kerala around June 1 before spreading nationwide by mid-July, allowing farmers to plant crops such as rice, corn, cotton, soybeans and sugarcane.

The onset of the southwest monsoon over Kerala on May 24 is its earliest onset since May 23, 2009, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) said on Saturday.

The monsoon has covered Kerala and parts of neighbouring Tamil Nadu and Karnataka, as well as parts of the northeastern state of Mizoram, the IMD said.

Conditions are favourable for the monsoon's further spread into Goa, parts of Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, the northeastern states, West Bengal, and the remaining parts of Karnataka and Tamil Nadu over the next 2 to 3 days.

Surplus pre-monsoon rainfall and an early monsoon onset will help farmers, especially in the southern and central states, to sow summer crops earlier than usual, said Ashwini Bansod, vice president for commodities research at Phillip Capital India, a Mumbai-based brokerage.

"Abundant soil moisture and early sowing could potentially boost crop yields," Bansod said.

Last year, the monsoon reached the coast of Kerala on May 30, and overall summer rains were the highest since 2020, supporting recovery from a drought in 2023.

The IMD last month forecast above-average monsoon rains for the second straight year in 2025.

The department defines average or normal rainfall as ranging between 96% and 104% of a 50-year average of 87 cm (35 inches) for the four-month season.

data.title: Ethereum Net Flows Turn Negative As Bulls Push For $3,500
data.description:
Ethereum net flows have been mostly negative for the last week, and a continuation of this suggests bullish momentum is building up for the price. These figures show how much buying and selling could’ve been going on and how investors are viewing the digital asset right now.

Ethereum Net Flows Bounce Around

Net flows refer to the difference in the number of coins entering or leaving crypto exchanges over a given period of time. If net flows are positive, then it means more ETH are flowing into crypto exchanges, which could point to sell-offs are investors take advantage of the price increase. However, if net flows are negative, then it suggests that more coins are leaving exchanges than those going in. Thus, it could point to buying pressure being higher than sell-offs.

Currently, Coinglass data shows that Ethereum net flows have been skewing toward negative for a while now. 24-hour net flow volumes show a -$182.86 million figure as more coins moved out of crypto exchanges during this time.

This has also persisted with six out of the last seven days showing that net flows are negative. This means that there have been more ETH leaving crypto exchanges than those going into exchanges for selling. Thus, showing that buying remains the order of the day. In this 7-day period, net flows for Ethereum are sitting at -$140 million.

However, going further back, on the 15-day timeframe, investors are still skewing more toward selling. This time period shows a positive $186.48 million in net flows as well, which would explain why the Ethereum price seems suppressed despite Bitcoin making new all-time highs..

The 30-day period is no different, showing even larger deposit trends. In total, Ethereum investors have moved more ETH into crypto exchanges, causing net flows to fall to rise to $483.54 million during this time.

What Could Happen To Price If Net Flows Remain Negative

If the Ethereum net flows continue to remain negative and even grow from here, then it would signal a rise in buying pressure. Once the buyers are able to outbid sellers, then the Ethereum price could begin to rally again.

As for how high the Ethereum price could go, crypto analyst Captain Faibik has explained that bulls are still struggling to reclaim the 200-Day Simple Moving Average at $27,000. Now, if they are able to capture this level and break out of it, the analyst sees the price rising above $3,500 in the near term.

data.title: Bitcoin treasury companies will hold 'way more' than Bitcoiners expect: Exec
data.description:
Moon Inc. head of Bitcoin strategy Jesse Myers says that Bitcoin holders are underestimating the significant amount of Bitcoin that corporations will accumulate by 2045.

“Bitcoin Treasury Companies will hold 50% of all BTC, way more than most Bitcoiners are prepared for,” Jesse Myers said in a May 23 X thread.

Strategy will own $70T of Bitcoin by 2025, says Myers

Myers further forecasted that Michael Saylor’s Strategy will own $70 trillion worth of Bitcoin by 2045, “making it by far the most valuable company in the history of the world.” At the time of publication, Strategy holds 576,320 Bitcoin, worth approximately $62.24 billion, according to Saylor Tracker.

Myers said, “To set the stage, there is $1,000T of asset value in the world.” He added that Bitcoin represents just 0.2% of that total amount. He explained that, since half of all capital in the world is essentially seeking the best store of value, that capital will “osmotically flow” into Bitcoin over time.

“Over the last 2 years, an exodus from fiat assets (bonds and money) has already begun. Hard money assets (Bitcoin and gold) are where things are shifting,” he said.

Myers said that there is around $318 trillion of capital in bonds “looking for greener pastures.” He said most of this capital is tied up in fixed-income institutional vehicles with “strict mandates.”

“That’s where Bitcoin Treasury Companies come in,” Myers said.

“Treasury Cos will be the primary bidders for BTC over the coming decades, deploying an ocean of SoV capital to BTC.”

While Strategy has been accumulating Bitcoin since 2020, other treasury companies are starting to emerge.

On April 24, Twenty One Capital formed, which is a Bitcoin treasury company led by Strike founder Jack Mallers with the support of Tether, SoftBank and Cantor Fitzgerald. The firm is looking to become the “superior vehicle for investors seeking capital-efficient Bitcoin exposure.”

According to Bitbo data, publicly traded and private companies, ETFs, and nation-states collectively hold 3.23 million BTC, valued at approximately $348.25 billion.

data.title: Judge overturns fraud convictions in Mango Markets exploit case
data.description:
A US federal judge has vacated key fraud and manipulation convictions against Avraham Eisenberg, the trader at the center of the case involving a $110 million exploit of the decentralized exchange Mango Markets.

On Friday, US District Judge Arun Subramanian ruled that the evidence presented at trial failed to support the jury’s conclusion that Eisenberg made materially false representations to Mango Markets.

The decision vacates Eisenberg’s convictions for commodities fraud and market manipulation and acquits him of a third charge, significantly weakening the government’s case.

Eisenberg, a self-proclaimed “applied game theorist,” was convicted in 2024 for artificially inflating the price of Mango’s MNGO token by over 1,300% in a matter of minutes and using the resulting gains as collateral to withdraw $110 million in crypto assets from the platform.

Judge sides with Eisenberg

The Justice Department argued that he deceived Mango’s smart contract-based lending system, but Eisenberg’s defense maintained that he merely exploited poorly designed, permissionless code — without making any false representations.

Judge Subramanian agreed, writing that “Mango Markets was permissionless and automatic,” meaning the system couldn’t be deceived in a legal sense. “There was insufficient evidence of falsity,” the judge added, siding with Eisenberg’s interpretation of DeFi mechanics.

The judge also rejected prosecutors’ argument that the case should be heard in New York. Eisenberg was in Puerto Rico at the time of the trades, and the court found that no meaningful activity tied to the alleged crime occurred in New York.

The DOJ had cited a Poughkeepsie-based Mango user and a third-party vendor in Manhattan, but the judge ruled these were not enough to establish proper venue.

The US government must now decide whether to refile the vacated charges, though the Trump administration has recently signaled a reduced focus on crypto enforcement. Eisenberg still faces civil suits from both the SEC and CFTC.

While this ruling clears Eisenberg in the Mango Markets case, he remains behind bars.

Eisenberg charged with child pornography

In a separate case, Eisenberg was sentenced to nearly four years in prison on May 1 after pleading guilty to possessing child pornography — a charge stemming from unrelated evidence uncovered during his arrest.

In December 2022, US federal law enforcement authorities arrested Eisenberg in Puerto Rico. FBI officials charged the hacker with one count of commodities fraud and one count of commodities manipulation.

A jury found Eisenberg guilty of wire fraud, commodities fraud, and commodities manipulation in April 2024. The defense argued that the exploit was not a cybercrime and represented a “successful and legal trading strategy.”

data.title: Retailers Are Giving Mixed Messages on Prices. What Will s Really Happen at Checkout. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Sabrina Escobar

Walmart and Home Depot offered shoppers two very different pictures of how President Donald Trump's new wave of tariffs will affect retail prices. Walmart said the levies would push prices higher, while Home Depot said they would be generally unchanged.

The difference in commentary reflects the tightrope retailers are walking: navigating a higher-tariff environment while coming off the heels of decades-high inflation. While raising prices could help these stores retain profits, it could also anger inflation-weary shoppers.

"It becomes a very challenging decision," said Ali Furman, consumer markets industry leader at PwC. "It isn't just a finance decision, it's a customer-relationship decision. And no retailer wants to be accused as gouging the customer in this current environment."

Days after Walmart's executive team talked about increasing prices, Trump posted on Truth Social that the company should "eat the tariffs," rather than raise prices. It's possible the posts prompted other retailers to take a more nuanced approach when talking about pricing to avoid incurring the president's wrath.

But that might be confusing the many consumers who are already seeking answers for how — and when — the new levies will affect their pocketbooks. The diverging answers from two of the country's largest retailers raises the question: Which is it? Are prices going up, or staying the same?

Unfortunately, the answer isn't straightforward. Few retailers — if any — will declare a blanket price increase and call it a day. Instead, most will combine pricing increases with a host of other strategies to offset the tariff impact and try to keep prices as low as possible, Furman said. And when they do decide to raise prices, they're going to be deliberate about it.

"You're not just going to peanut butter your pricing strategy across your whole product portfolio," Furman said.

Plus, retailers will have different capabilities to absorb the tariffs based on their business model, reliance on heavily tariffed imports, and supply chain strategy. For instance, home improvement stores like Home Depot will have "more wiggle room" than grocers to keep prices low, wrote Carol Levenson, an analyst at research firm Gimme Credit.

Groceries and other essentials are lower-margin items than appliances and other products sold by home improvement retailers. Home Depot's adjusted operating margin for the fiscal year ended February was 13.5%, while Walmart's was 4.4%.

These factors mean pricing strategies will end up looking different across companies. That said, many approaches will might end up looking a lot like that of Target, which said continually adjusting prices is part of its regular functions.

"We look at competition and we make adjustments literally each and every week," said Target CEO Brian Cornell on a call with media. "So we're constantly adjusting pricing, some are going up, some will be reduced, but that's an ongoing effort that takes place each and every day."

Even Home Depot, which has said it aims to "generally maintain" current pricing levels, expects to see some fluctuation.

"It's common to see some shifts in pricing, which are part of the normal course of business, but it's important to remember that as our customer's advocate for value we manage pricing on a portfolio basis across the store," said Home Depot spokeswoman Beth Marlowe in emailed comments to Barron's.

The so-called portfolio pricing approach considers all of the retailer's product range when setting prices — rather than pricing each item individually. The goal isn't necessarily to maximize profit on every product. Some, for example, might be priced at a loss to simply drive traffic, and others might be priced higher to protect margins.

That pricing strategy was already on display weeks ahead of Home Depot's earnings. Barron's analyzed weekly pricing data, provided by data company OpenBrand, for seven appliances sold by Home Depot, dating back to the first week of January through early May.

Some prices have remained generally unchanged — the $129.99 final price tag for a Ninja air fryer has been the same since the week of Jan. 12 through May 11, even though the average price for that air fryer across other retailers has gone up about 4%.

Other appliances, however, have seen big jumps. The shelf price (the list price before any promotions) for an LG front-loading washing machine ticked up about 5% to $1249 the week of May 11 from $1,199 in the first week of January, according to OpenBrand.

The net price (after promotions and any other discounts) of that same washer has fluctuated even more, jumping by about 56% to $1,249 from $798 over that span. A Whirlpool dishwasher's net price rose by 6.4%, on average, in the same time frame.

Whirlpool executives said on the company's April earnings call that the company had "successfully implemented pricing actions" that month to offset higher tariffs on machine parts — on top of several increases over the past 12 months. LG said it had a "road map" for raising prices in the second quarter.

So, will prices go up? The highly unsatisfying conclusion is, it depends on where you shop, what you're buying, and when you're buying it. Time to fire up those price comparison tools.

Write to Sabrina Escobar at sabrina.escobar@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
